title: Prostate Cancer: Screening, Diagnosis, and Management
objectives: Understand PSA screening recommendations and shared decision-making
Interpret Gleason grading and risk stratification
Apply appropriate management strategies by risk group
Recognize treatment complications and side effects

sections.title: Epidemiology
sections.content:
**Most common non-cutaneous cancer in men**

• Lifetime risk: 1 in 8
• Second leading cause of cancer death in men
• Median age at diagnosis: 66 years

**Risk Factors:**
• Age (strongest factor)
• African American race (2x incidence, higher mortality)
• Family history (BRCA1/2, Lynch syndrome)
• High-fat diet (controversial)

sections.title: PSA Screening
sections.content:
**PSA Basics:**
• Prostate-specific, NOT cancer-specific
• Elevated in BPH, prostatitis, instrumentation, ejaculation

**Screening Recommendations (USPSTF/AUA):**
• Age 55-69: Shared decision-making
• Age <55: Only if high risk
• Age >70: Generally not recommended

**PSA Interpretation:**
• Traditional cutoff: 4.0 ng/mL
• PSA density: PSA/prostate volume
• PSA velocity: >0.75 ng/mL/year concerning
• Free/Total PSA: Lower ratio = higher cancer risk

sections.title: Diagnosis & Grading
sections.content:
**Biopsy Indications:**
• Elevated PSA
• Abnormal DRE
• Rising PSA

**Gleason Grading:**
• Primary + Secondary pattern = Gleason Score
• Grade Groups 1-5 (new system)
• Group 1: Gleason 6 (3+3)
• Group 2: Gleason 7 (3+4)
• Group 3: Gleason 7 (4+3)
• Group 4: Gleason 8
• Group 5: Gleason 9-10

**Key Concept:** 3+4 ≠ 4+3 (4+3 is worse)

sections.title: Risk Stratification
sections.content:
**Low Risk:**
• PSA <10, Gleason ≤6, Stage T1-T2a
• Options: Active surveillance, radiation, surgery

**Intermediate Risk:**
• PSA 10-20, Gleason 7, or Stage T2b-T2c
• Options: Radiation ± ADT, surgery

**High Risk:**
• PSA >20, Gleason 8-10, or Stage T3+
• Options: Radiation + long-term ADT, surgery ± adjuvant therapy

**Metastatic:**
• ADT backbone
• Docetaxel, abiraterone, enzalutamide

sections.title: Treatment Options
sections.content:
**Active Surveillance:**
• For low-risk disease
• Regular PSA, DRE, repeat biopsies
• Avoids treatment side effects

**Radical Prostatectomy:**
• Surgical removal of prostate
• Complications: ED (30-70%), incontinence (5-20%)

**Radiation Therapy:**
• EBRT or brachytherapy
• Similar efficacy to surgery for localized disease
• Complications: Bowel/bladder irritation, ED

**Androgen Deprivation Therapy:**
• GnRH agonists (leuprolide) or antagonists
• Anti-androgens (bicalutamide, enzalutamide)
• Side effects: Hot flashes, osteoporosis, metabolic syndrome

sections.title: Follow-up & Recurrence
sections.content:
**Post-Treatment PSA:**
• After prostatectomy: Should be undetectable
• After radiation: Nadir + 2.0 = biochemical recurrence

**Biochemical Recurrence:**
• PSA rises before clinical recurrence
• Imaging workup (PSMA-PET, bone scan)
• Salvage options: Radiation, ADT

**Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer:**
• Progression despite testosterone <50 ng/dL
• Multiple treatment options available
• Multidisciplinary approach

keyTakeaways: Active surveillance is appropriate for low-risk disease
Treatment decisions based on risk stratification

references.citation: AUA Guideline: Clinically Localized Prostate Cancer

reviewQuestions.question: A 62-year-old man has a prostate biopsy showing Gleason 3+4=7 in 2 of 12 cores. PSA is 8 ng/mL. DRE shows no nodules. What is his risk category?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Intermediate risk is defined by PSA 10-20 OR Gleason 7 OR stage T2b-T2c. His Gleason 7 places him in intermediate risk despite PSA <10.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected prostate cancer?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in prostate cancer. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of prostate cancer. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing prostate cancer, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
